content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street ♦ Suite 620 ♦ Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 ♦ Fax (916) 322-0886

April 20, 2004

Timothy W. Boyer
State Board of Equalization
Post Office Box 942879
Sacramento, CA 94279-0083

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-080

Dear Mr. Boyer:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of the State Board of Equalization for advice regarding the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Is the "Prosecuting Tax Evasion" handbook issued by the Board of Equalization, and distributed to other governmental agencies and officials (i.e., the Office of the Attorney General, district attorneys, city attorneys and superior courts) exempt from the prohibition of sending of mass mailings at public expense?

CONCLUSION

Items sent in the normal course of business from one governmental entity or officer to another governmental entity or officer are exempt from the restrictions of section 89001.

FACTS

The Board of Equalization works closely with law enforcement agencies in the prosecution of tax evasion cases. Your enclosed handbook and CD were developed as a multi-media resource for California's prosecuting attorneys handling tax evasion cases on behalf of the Board of Equalization. The handbook and CD provide information to prosecutors handling cases that are outside of their normal areas of expertise. You believe that it is important for agencies to provide comprehensive prosecution materials to prosecuting attorneys in order for them to undertake the prosecution of criminal offenses,

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No.A-04-080
Page No. 2

such as tax evasion or tax fraud, sections which are found primarily outside of the Penal
Code. The Board of Equalization seeks to make prosecution of tax evasion cases as
efficient and effective as possible by providing your expertise and full cooperation to
prosecuting attorneys. The handbook will be distributed to other governmental agencies
and officials only, i.e. the Office of the Attorney General, district attorneys, city attorneys
and superior courts.

Absent an exception to the mass mailing prohibition, the items you enclosed
would constitute a prohibited mass mailing because they feature members of the Board of
Equalization. In particular, the CD has a taped introduction by Boardmember John
Chiang and his name also appears in the acknowledgement segment of the CD. The
handbook features all the members of the Board of Equalization under the tab "State
Board of Equalization." Finally, the cover letter that will accompany the package will
include Boardmember Chiang's signature.

ANALYSIS

The Mass Mailings Prohibition

In June 1988, Proposition 73 amended section 89001 of the Act to provide: "No
newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public expense." Regulation 18901(a)
clarifies that a mailing is prohibited only if all of the following apply:

"(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at
his or her residence, place of employment or business, or post office box.
For purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered to the recipient
must be a tangible item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written
document.

"(2) The item sent either:

"(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, or

"(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to
an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the
mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination,
or concert with the elected officer.

"(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public
moneys; or

"(B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $50.00
are paid with public moneys, and the design, production, or printing is
done with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.

File No.A-04-080
Page No. 3

"(4) More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in
a single calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in subdivision (b)."

In your letter requesting advice, you acknowledge that the item will be considered
a "prohibited mass mailing" under the criteria in regulation 18901(a). Your questions
concerns the exceptions to the mass mailing prohibition in regulation 18901(b).

Exceptions

As stated above, regulation 18901 provides exemptions whereby an otherwise
prohibited newsletter or mass mailing may be distributed. Regulation 18901 exempts
intergovernmental and intra-governmental mailings sent in the normal course of business.
(Regulation 18901(b)(3) and 18901 (b)(4).) Therefore, information sent in the normal
course of business to other governmental entities or officers, or to intra-agency members
and officials, which features elected officials, are not prohibited by the Act. (Hamby
Advice Letter, No. A-88-476; Waggoner Advice Letter, No. A-92-247.)

Mailings to employees and other governmental entities fall within this exception
so long as they are sent in the normal course of business.² (Mott-Smith Advice Letter,
No. I-89-108.) In light of the fact that the handbook is distributed to the Office of the
Attorney General, district attorneys, city attorneys and superior courts and bears a
reasonable relationship to the functions carried out by your office (to provide
comprehensive materials in connection with prosecution of criminal violations of the
state's tax laws), we conclude the handbook qualifies under the exception listed above.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel

JWW:jg
I:\AdviceLetrs\04-080

² Please note that this exception is limited to mailings to another governmental entity or officer.
(Mott-Smith Advice Letter, supra.) Moreover, the advice herein would not apply if the materials were
distributed to other government agencies with the intent that it be redistributed to a total of more than 200
additional persons at their residences, businesses, post office boxes, or places of employment. A
redistribution of this nature could well result in a violation of the mass mailing prohibition. (Symkowick
Advice Letter, No. A-97-178.)

Items sent in the normal course of business from one governmental entity or
officer to another govemmental entity or officer are exempt from the restrictions of

and distributed to other govemmental agencies and officials (i.e., the Office oith"
Attorney General, district attorneys, city attorneys and superior courts) exernpt from the
prohibition of sending of mass mailings at public expense?

CONCLUSION: Items sent in the normal course of business from one governmental entity or
officer to another govemmental entity or officer are exempt from the restrictions of
section 89001.
